The remaining 0.8% of the body's store of elements consists of 14 elements known as the *trace elements*. These include aluminium (Al), boron (B), chromium (Cr), cobalt (Co), copper (Cu), fluorine (F), iodine (I), manganese (Mn), molybdenum (Mo), selenium (Se), silicon (Si), tin (Sn), vanadium (V) and zinc (Zn).

Elements are important for human life. They are components of the energy sources of carbohydrates, fats and proteins, and they also provide us with water and important vitamins and minerals that are needed to sustain healthy living. An overview of the main chemical elements within the human body is shown in Table 3.1.

## Definition

Trace elements are the 14 elements that together make up the remaining 0.8% of the body's total store of elements, present in very small quantities yet essential to human life. They include aluminium (Al), boron (B), chromium (Cr), cobalt (Co), copper (Cu), fluorine (F), iodine (I), manganese (Mn), molybdenum (Mo), selenium (Se), silicon (Si), tin (Sn), vanadium (V) and zinc (Zn).

## Core Principles

- Although trace elements collectively account for only 0.8% of the body's elemental composition, they are indispensable components of the biochemical machinery that sustains life.
- Elements in the body serve as constituents of the energy substrates carbohydrates, fats and proteins, and supply water along with vitamins and minerals required for healthy living.

## Sub-concepts

- The 14 trace elements: aluminium (Al), boron (B), chromium (Cr), cobalt (Co), copper (Cu), fluorine (F), iodine (I), manganese (Mn), molybdenum (Mo), selenium (Se), silicon (Si), tin (Sn), vanadium (V) and zinc (Zn).

## Practical Applications

- Adequate intake of trace elements through diet contributes to sustaining healthy living, supporting the supply of vitamins and minerals derived from food alongside carbohydrates, fats, proteins and water.
